Who I Am

A Researcher at the Intersection of Engineering & Medicine

I am a Mechanical Engineer and Research Assistant at the Laboratory of Biomechanics, UET Lahore, specialising in cardiovascular biomechanics and advanced computational modelling. My work develops high-fidelity numerical frameworks that capture the complex interplay between arterial mechanics, haemodynamics, and clinical measurement.

Through active collaboration with the University of Manchester's School of Mechanical, Aerospace and Civil Engineering, and clinical partnership with Stockport NHS Foundation Trust, I contribute to research bridging theoretical biomechanics with real-world clinical validation — improving non-invasive blood pressure measurement for all patient populations.

My research has been presented at leading international congresses including ISB Stockholm 2025, ESB Zurich 2025, IEEE EMBC Denmark 2025, and WCB Vancouver 2026.

Academic Output

Research &
Publications

My research investigates the biomechanical factors governing non-invasive blood pressure measurement. Through a combination of analytical, finite element, and two-way fluid-structure interaction modelling — validated against NHS clinical data — I seek to improve oscillometric BP accuracy across all patient populations, including the elderly, obese, and those with vascular disease.

Pulsatile Blood Flow Velocity Field — 2-Way FSI
Wall Shear Stress Distribution
Upper Arm Finite Element Mesh
Pressure Distribution — Stenotic Aorta
Oscillometric Envelopes — Arterial Stiffness Study

Evidence from the Research

Key Research
Findings

Drawn from finite element analysis, fluid-structure interaction modelling, and clinical validation — these findings reveal how biomechanical factors fundamentally affect blood pressure measurement accuracy, with direct implications for device design and clinical practice in hypertension management.

FSI Framework · Journal of Biomechanics

Non-Linear Two-Way FSI Model Achieves Clinical-Grade Accuracy in Oscillometric BP Simulation

A non-linear two-way fluid-structure interaction framework integrating pulsatile haemodynamics, Carreau non-Newtonian blood rheology, hyperelastic arterial mechanics (Ogden N=2), and soft tissue deformation was developed in ANSYS Fluent + Transient Structural and validated against Stockport NHS clinical data. The model reproduces physiologically realistic oscillometric waveforms with MAP accuracy within 2% — establishing a rigorous computational basis for future in-silico BP device testing across diverse patient populations.

2%Validation vs NHS clinical data
94.30mmHg MAP predicted (actual: 94.99)
537KFluid mesh elements at convergence
👴
FEA Parametric Study · ISB 2025 Stockholm · Abaqus/CAE

Ageing Introduces Up to 20% Error in Oscillometric BP Estimation

Systematic FEA sensitivity analysis demonstrated that age-related increases in arterial wall stiffness (Ogden N=6, Bank 1995/1996 brachial artery data) and surrounding tissue stiffness cause characteristic ratios to vary from 0.54–0.91 for SBP and 0.66–0.95 for DBP. Devices using fixed constant ratios systematically misclassify blood pressure in elderly patients — a primary mechanism underlying pseudo-hypertension in automated oscillometric devices.

↑ 20% max BP estimation error in elderly populations
🔬
Hyperelastic Model Study · Chapter 6 · Abaqus/CAE

Only Ogden N=6 Correctly Reproduces the Oscillometric Waveform

Systematic evaluation of Neo-Hookean, Mooney-Rivlin, Yeoh, Reduced Polynomial (N=2–6), and Ogden (N=1–6) formulations showed that only Ogden N=6 captures the critical non-linear stress-strain transition of the brachial artery beyond ε = 0.2 — the key determinant of the characteristic bell-shaped oscillometric waveform. Previous FEA studies using Neo-Hookean were fundamentally flawed: the model produces an increasing oscillation amplitude rather than the physiologically correct rise-and-fall envelope.

Neo-Hookean fails to model artery behaviour above ε = 0.2
💧
FSI Chapter 9 · ANSYS Fluent — Haemodynamics

Cuff Pressure Paradoxically Decreases Blood Velocity — Not Increases It

Counter to classical rigid-tube Bernoulli intuition (v = Q/A), applying cuff pressure reduces blood velocity. As pc increases, the compliant arterial wall absorbs strain energy and increases vascular resistance, reducing flow. Two-way FSI revealed velocity dropping from 128 cm/s to 62 cm/s under full cuff pressure — a collapsiblity-driven mechanism absent in rigid-wall and one-way FSI models, with direct implications for oscillometric BP signal interpretation.

Peak velocity: 128 cm/s → 62 cm/s under full cuff pressure
98%
Max PTR at cuff centre
FEA + FSI · Chapter 6 & FSI Paper · Abaqus & ANSYS
Non-Uniform Pressure Transmission Explains Obesity-Related BP Overestimation

Pressure transmission reaches ~98% at the cuff centre but drops below 40% at the edges — a non-uniformity driven by soft tissue deformation mechanics. In obese subjects with a 20 mm fat layer, maximum pressure transmission reduces from 147 mmHg to 125 mmHg. This mechanically explains why oscillometric devices systematically overestimate BP in obese patients and underscores the need for patient-specific cuff calibration algorithms.

−1.75
mmHg Buckling Onset
FEA · Chapter 6 · Abaqus/CAE — Artery Closure Study
Arterial Buckling Threshold Is Linearly Linked to Soft Tissue Stiffness

Isolated brachial artery begins buckling at transmural pressure of −1.75 mmHg and fully collapses at −3.2 mmHg — consistent with tube law. When surrounded by soft tissue, buckling shifts to −63 to −125 mmHg with a linear relationship to tissue stiffness (ET = 30–55 kPa). This tissue-mediated protection of the artery is a critical factor governing oscillometric waveform generation and must be incorporated into BP device models.

🔄
FSI Validation · Chapter 9 · ANSYS One-Way vs Two-Way

One-Way FSI Fundamentally Misrepresents Arterial Haemodynamics

Direct comparative simulation showed one-way FSI produces unrealistic diameter oscillations that fade during diastole — treating the artery effectively as a rigid structure with no fluid feedback. Two-way FSI correctly captures bidirectional coupling, producing physiologically accurate velocity profiles, realistic wall deformation throughout the full cardiac cycle, and correct oscillometric waveform generation. One-way FSI should not be used in cardiovascular FSI studies without critical justification.

1-Way FSI overestimates peak velocity; misses diastolic dynamics
📈
Multiphysics Model · ISB 2025 & ESB 2025 Conferences

Pulse Pressure Is the Dominant Driver of BP Estimation Error — 30% Ratio Shift

Varying pulse pressure from 40 to 80 mmHg caused systolic characteristic ratios to shift from 0.50 to 0.79 — a 30% variation that dwarfs the effect of heart rate (below 3%). Diastolic ratios varied up to 15%. This makes pulse pressure the single most clinically significant parameter for oscillometric algorithm accuracy, ahead of arterial stiffness and tissue mechanics — with critical implications for patients with wide pulse pressure such as isolated systolic hypertension.

Pulse pressure dominates: 30% systolic ratio shift (vs 3% heart rate)
🫀
Carotid FSI · WCB 2026 Vancouver · Lead Author

Novel Analytical FSI Framework Validated Within 2% for Carotid Artery Dynamics

A novel analytical fluid-structure interaction framework for common carotid artery diameter variation over the full cardiac cycle was developed and validated against experimental data at all measured time points within 2%. ANSYS two-way FSI replicated analytical carotid diameter profiles with strong agreement, confirming model fidelity for future patient-specific vascular studies. The framework provides a computationally efficient alternative to full numerical FSI for routine clinical assessment.

±2% validation vs experimental data at all cardiac cycle time points
4.5
Pa Peak WSS
FSI Study · Chapter 9 · ANSYS Fluent — Haemodynamic Analysis
Wall Shear Stress Dynamics Reveal Atherosclerosis Risk Mechanisms During BP Measurement

Two-way FSI revealed peak wall shear stress of ~4.5 Pa during systole, with flow reversal and near-zero WSS in early diastole. Chronic low-WSS exposure causes endothelial cells to adopt pro-atherogenic behaviour, promoting plaque accumulation — particularly relevant in patients with narrow velocity profiles (Cases I–V). In high-pulse-pressure patients (Case VI), elevated WSS may amplify mechanotransductive signals, altering oxygen transport and inflammatory mediator distribution. These findings connect BP measurement haemodynamics directly to long-term cardiovascular risk stratification.

Selected Work

Research Projects

Cardiovascular · 2-Way FSI · FEA · Clinical Validation

Cuff-Based Oscillometric Blood Pressure Modelling via FSI

High-fidelity non-linear two-way FSI framework for oscillometric BP measurement integrating Carreau non-Newtonian blood rheology and Ogden N=2 hyperelastic arterial model. Quantified ageing, cuff fabric stiffness, and non-linear waveform effects. Validated against Stockport NHS clinical data within 2% accuracy. MAP estimated at 94.30 mmHg vs actual 94.99 mmHg.

ANSYS FluentANSYS MechanicalAbaqus2-Way FSIPython
Vascular · Analytical FSI · Cardiac Cycle · WCB 2026

Carotid Artery Diameter Variation — Analytical FSI Framework

Novel analytical FSI framework for common carotid artery diameter variation over a pulsatile cardiac cycle. Achieved validation within 2% of experimental data at all measured time points. Provides a computationally efficient alternative to full numerical FSI. Accepted as Lead Author at WCB 2026, Vancouver.

ANSYS FluentAnalytical FSIMATLABPython
CFD · Patient-Specific · Atherosclerosis · 2-Way FSI

Patient-Specific FSI — Blood Flow in Stenotic Aorta

Two-way coupled FSI simulation of pulsatile blood flow in healthy, 30% and 40% stenosis aortic models over a full 0.75s cardiac cycle. The 40% stenosis model showed wall deformation of 1.66 mm and peak velocity of 2.16 m/s. Identified high-risk regions for cholesterol accumulation and atherosclerosis development.

ANSYS FluentANSYS Mechanical2-Way FSICFD
Orthopaedic · FEA · Reverse Engineering · 3D Scanning

Total Knee Replacement Implant — 3D Scanned FEA

Anatomically accurate TKR implant model via reverse engineering of 3D scanned data achieving geometric accuracy within ±0.001 mm. FEA on CoCrMo femoral component and UHMWPE tibial insert under 1.8 kN static normal load; obtained max contact pressure of 15.28 MPa — within 10% of published benchmark value of 16.99 MPa.

AbaqusSolidWorks3D SlicerFEA
Clinical · Ageing · FEA Parametric Study · Algorithm Design

Effect of Ageing on Oscillometric BP Measurement Accuracy

Comprehensive FEA parametric study using Bank 1995 and Bank 1996 brachial artery data showing age-related arterial and tissue stiffening produces up to 20% BP estimation error with constant-ratio algorithms. Provided specific design considerations: characteristic ratio range of 0.62–0.91 for SBP and 0.79–0.92 for DBP must be accounted for in next-generation devices.

AbaqusFEAMATLABSensitivity Analysis
Orthopaedic · FEA · Implant Design · Univ. Manchester

Humeral & Femoral Bone Implant Biomechanics

Collaboration with University of Manchester on biomechanics of humeral and femoral bone implants under physiological loading. Supporting implant design optimisation for reduced stress shielding and improved long-term performance. Resulted in 2 conference submissions. Mentored 5 final-year undergraduate students on FEA methodology for orthopaedic applications.
